statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Define $ f(t) $ as the maximum number of parts $ t $ can be split into such that each part is a non-empty prefix of $ t $ . In other words, $ f(t) $ is the maximum positive integer $ k $ that satisfies the following condition:

- There exist $ k $ strings $ p_1,p_2,\ldots,p_k $ , which are prefixes of $ t $ , such that $ t=p_1+p_2+\ldots+p_k $ . Here $ + $ denotes the string concatenation.

You are given a string $ s $ of length $ n $ , consisting of only lowercase English letters. Let $ s[x..y] $ represent the substring $ ^{\text{∗}} $ of $ s $ from the $ x $ -th to the $ y $ -th character (inclusive). You need to answer $ q $ queries. The $ i $ -th query provides two integers $ l_i $ and $ r_i $ , and you need to find

$$$
\sum_{j=l_i}^{r_i} f(s[l_i..j]).
$$$

$ ^{\text{∗}} $ A string $ a $ is a substring of a string $ b $ if $ a $ can be obtained from $ b $ by the deletion of several (possibly, zero or all) characters from the beginning and several (possibly, zero or all) characters from the end.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^3 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains two integers $ n $ and $ q $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 10^6 $ , $ 1 \le q \le 100 $ ).

The second line contains the string $ s $ of length $ n $ , consisting of only lowercase English letters.

The $ i $ -th of the next $ q $ lines contains two integers $ l_i $ and $ r_i $ ( $ 1 \le l_i \le r_i \le n $ ), representing the $ i $ -th query.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 10^6 $ .

Output Format
For each query, output an integer representing the value of the expression.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
6
1 1
a
1 1
5 2
aaaaa
1 5
2 4
6 2
abcdef
1 6
3 5
6 3
abaaba
1 6
1 3
2 6
7 3
abcabca
1 7
2 7
4 7
8 3
aababaac
1 8
2 8
3 7
Output
1
15
6
6
3
14
4
7
12
9
5
13
14
7

Note
In the first test case, $ f(\texttt{a})=1 $ .

In the second test case, $ f(\texttt{a})+f(\texttt{aa})+f(\texttt{aaa})+f(\texttt{aaaa})+f(\texttt{aaaaa})=1+2+3+4+5=15 $ and $ f(\texttt{a})+f(\texttt{aa})+f(\texttt{aaa})=1+2+3=6 $ .

In the third test case, $ f(\texttt{a})+f(\texttt{ab})+f(\texttt{abc})+f(\texttt{abcd})+f(\texttt{abcde})+f(\texttt{abcdef})=1+1+1+1+1+1=6 $ and $ f(\texttt{c})+f(\texttt{cd})+f(\texttt{cde})=1+1+1=3 $ .

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2209E - A Trivial String Problem
Firstly, some basic knowledge we need for the solution:
A
border
of a string $$$s$$$ is a non-empty string $$$t$$$ such that $$$|t| \lt |s|$$$, and $$$t$$$ is simultaneously a prefix and a suffix of $$$s$$$. For example, both $$$\texttt{a}$$$ and $$$\texttt{aba}$$$ are borders of $$$\texttt{abacaba}$$$; $$$\texttt{aaa}$$$ is a border of $$$\texttt{aaaa}$$$. If a string does not have any borders, we call it
unbordered
; otherwise, we call it
bordered
. For example, $$$\texttt{abaabb}$$$ is unbordered.
If $$$c$$$ is a border of $$$b$$$, and $$$b$$$ is a border of $$$a$$$, then $$$c$$$ is a border of $$$a$$$. This can be easily derived from the definition of borders.
Consider all borders of a string $$$s$$$. Let them be $$$b_1,b_2,\ldots,b_k$$$ when sorted in increasing order of their lengths. Then for every $$$1 \le i  \lt  k$$$, $$$b_i$$$ is a border of $$$b_{i+1}$$$.
Now, we will derive the solution step by step. A partition will be referred to as
optimal
if it contains the most number of substrings.
Lemma 1.
For a string $$$s$$$, it is either unbordered, or its shortest border is unbordered.
Proof.
Suppose $$$s$$$ is bordered, and its shortest border $$$t$$$ is also bordered. Then, there exists a border $$$w$$$ of $$$t$$$, which will also be a border of $$$s$$$, contradicting the fact that $$$t$$$ is the shortest border of $$$s$$$.
Lemma 2.
For a string $$$s$$$, there exists
one and only one
partition of $$$s$$$ into its unbordered prefixes.
Proof.
We shall prove by induction. Let a possible partition be $$$s=t_1+t_2+\ldots+t_k$$$. Consider two cases:
If $$$s$$$ is unbordered, the only possible partition is $$$s$$$ itself. This is because the last substring, $$$t_k$$$, must either be $$$s$$$ or a border of $$$s$$$ (prefix by definition of partition, suffix by being the last substring).
Otherwise, since $$$t_k$$$ is an unbordered border of $$$s$$$, it is the shortest border of $$$s$$$. Thus, the last substring of the partition is fixed. This reduces the problem to partitioning $$$t_1+t_2+\ldots+t_{k-1}$$$.
Corollary 3.
The unique partition of $$$s$$$ into its unbordered prefixes can be obtained by iteratively trimming the shortest border of $$$s$$$ until $$$s$$$ is unbordered.
Lemma 4.
In an optimal partition of $$$s$$$ into its prefixes (
not necessarily unbordered
), all substrings must be unbordered.
Proof.
If any $$$t_i$$$ in the partition of $$$s$$$ is bordered, we can partition it further into the unbordered prefixes of $$$t_i$$$, which will also be unbordered prefixes of $$$s$$$.
Theorem.
The
unique optimal
partition of $$$s$$$ into its prefixes can be obtained by iteratively trimming the shortest border of $$$s$$$ until $$$s$$$ is unbordered.
Proof.
All substrings in the optimal partition are unbordered (Lemma 4) $$$\to$$$ There exists a unique partition into unbordered prefixes (Lemma 2) $$$\to$$$ This partition can be obtained by iteratively trimming the shortest border (Corollary 3).
Now we will discuss how to implement this algorithm efficiently. Notice that the constraints allow a $$$O(nq)$$$ solution, so we can treat each query independently. Thus, we will discuss how to solve the problem for a single string $$$s$$$.
Firstly, compute the $$$\pi$$$ array of $$$s$$$ using KMP, where $$$\pi_i$$$ is the length of the longest border of $$$s[1..i]$$$. However, instead of the longest border, we need the shortest one. Let $$$b_i$$$ be the length of the shortest border of $$$s[1..i]$$$, or $$$0$$$ if no border exists. $$$b$$$ can also be easily computed by a simple linear scan:
$$$$$$b_i= \begin{cases} 0& \pi_i=0 \\ \pi_i & \pi_{\pi_i}=0 \\ b_{\pi_i} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$$$$$
Lastly, the $$$f$$$ value of all prefixes of $$$s$$$ can be computed by another linear scan. Let $$$dp_i=f(s[1..i])$$$:
$$$$$$dp_i= \begin{cases} 1 & b_i = 0 \\ dp_{i-b_i}+1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$$$$$
As such, we have solved the problem in $$$O(nq)$$$ time.
